summary: Matched Mr. Game & Watch's `ftGw_SpecialN_CreateSausage` by replacing a large `MUST_MATCH` assembly block and previously nonmatching C attempt with structured C that preserves the needed stack/register behavior. The fix introduced a larger `ftGameWatchChef` helper struct, used nested scopes and deliberate unused stack padding under `MUST_MATCH`, adjusted the `it_802C837C` prototype to use `enum_t` for the item kind parameter, and made minor include-format whitespace changes in capsule/parasol item files.
what_changed: Removed the hand-written `static asm void ftGw_SpecialN_CreateSausage(HSD_GObj* gobj)` implementation guarded by `MUST_MATCH`.

Reworked the C implementation of `ftGw_SpecialN_CreateSausage` to match, including nested scopes, local `Vec3 vec0`/`vec1`, `ftGameWatchChef chefStruct`, `fp1 = gobj->user_data`, pointer-based filling of `chefStruct.sausageCount`, and preserved assignment ordering around the chef history variables.
Kept deliberate unused stack padding under `#ifdef MUST_MATCH`, changing it from a single `u8 _[8]` to multiple scoped arrays including `u8 _[4] = { 0 }` and `u8 _[8] = { 0 }`.
Changed `ftGameWatchChef::sausageCount` from `s32 sausageCount[5]` to `int sausageCount[6]`, apparently to satisfy stack/layout requirements used by the matching function.
Changed `it_802C837C`'s third parameter in `it_27CF.h` from `s32` to `enum_t`, while leaving the call in this function as `it_802C837C(gobj, &vec0, 122, var_r6, fp->facing_dir)`.
Added blank lines after `#include "math.h"` in `itcapsule.c` and `itparasol.c`, likely formatting or include grouping cleanup.
smart_moves: Used a small helper struct, `ftGameWatchChef`, for the sausage candidate array instead of a plain local array, giving the compiler a stack layout closer to the target.
Used nested blocks to influence local lifetimes and stack allocation without resorting to inline assembly.
Preserved suspicious source patterns that affect codegen, such as `for (i = 5, i -= 5; i < 5; i++)` instead of a simpler `for (i = 0; i < 5; i++)`.
Filled sausage candidates via an `int* pSausage` pointer and incremented `var_r28`, matching the target's compact loop behavior more closely than indexed array writes.
Separated `temp_r5` and `var_r6` assignments before the `it_802C837C` call, preserving the original value-flow/order needed for register allocation.
Used `fp1 = gobj->user_data` separately from the earlier `GET_FIGHTER(gobj)` access, which mirrors the assembly's reload/use pattern rather than over-simplifying all fighter references.
Removed obsolete decomp.me scratch commentary once the function matched, reducing stale uncertainty in the source.
decomp_lessons: For stubborn matching functions, nested scopes and intentionally scoped dummy stack arrays can be more effective than trying to force matches through inlines alone.
A local helper struct can be used as a stack-shaping device; here, `ftGameWatchChef` with `sausageCount[6]` replaced a direct `sausageCount[5]` local to achieve the desired layout.
Do not simplify odd-looking initialization or loop expressions if they help reproduce target codegen; the `i = 5, i -= 5` form is likely deliberate.
When an assembly block loads fighter data more than once, matching C may need to preserve separate local fighter pointers or access paths instead of reusing a single canonical local.

The exact order of state updates matters: this function first copies `x2240_chefVar1` into `x2244_chefVar2`, then writes the newly selected sausage into `x2240_chefVar1`, then passes the selected value to item creation.
Header type refinements can be part of a function match; changing an item creation parameter from `s32` to `enum_t` documents intent and may improve callsite typing.
naming_conventions: `ftGw_SpecialN_CreateSausage` follows the Game & Watch fighter prefix `ftGw_` and special-move naming pattern.
`ftGameWatchChef` and `ftGameWatchJudge` are fighter-specific helper typedef names in `ftGameWatch/types.h`.
Game & Watch fighter variables retain field-style names such as `x2240_chefVar1` and `x2244_chefVar2`, indicating partially identified struct members with offset-derived prefixes.
Move variables use `fp->mv.gw.SpecialN.maxSausage`; command variables use `fp->cmd_vars[0]`.

Temporary locals in this match use generic codegen-oriented names such as `vec0`, `vec1`, `fp1`, `var_r28`, `temp_r5`, and `var_r6`, suggesting register/stack matching took priority over semantic naming.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Converted a `MUST_MATCH` assembly implementation into matching C.
Used `#ifdef MUST_MATCH` dummy unused stack arrays to control stack frame shape.
Introduced nested lexical scopes to control local variable lifetime and placement.
Used a stack helper struct with a six-element `int` array despite only iterating over five sausage IDs.
Used pointer increment writes (`*pSausage++ = i`) rather than direct indexed stores.
Preserved branch and state-update behavior around `fp->cmd_vars[0]`, `maxSausage`, `accessory4_cb = NULL`, and the item spawn call.
Kept literal float local vector initialization with `2.5f`, `6.5f`, and `0.0f`, then transformed from the left-thumb joint using `lb_8000B1CC`.
follow_up_queries: Look for other Game & Watch Chef functions that use `ftGameWatchChef` or `sausageCount[6]` for stack layout.
Audit callsites and implementation of `it_802C837C` to confirm the `enum_t` item-kind parameter naming and possible constants for literal `122`.
Search for remaining `MUST_MATCH` dummy stack arrays in fighter code to reuse this nested-scope stack-shaping pattern.
Investigate whether `x2240_chefVar1` and `x2244_chefVar2` can be given semantic names now that their role as recent sausage selections is clearer.
